## S1.04 Intro to emails {#s1.04-intro-to-emails}

**Owner:** Directors · **Last reviewed:** 2025 board (imported from the Hitchhiker's Guide, July 2026) · **Status:** current — when you find something stale, fix it and update this line.

### Checking the gmail {#checking-the-gmail}

You and your co-director will need to set up a plan for who will respond to which emails. Some directors trade off who is in charge of responding to email every day, while others have one director check in the morning and the other in the afternoon. Either way is fine, as long as emails are responded to in a timely manner. Additionally, checking the gmail and responding to emails is a task that should be evenly split between the directors, so if one system isn’t working out, then feel free to switch how emails are divided. All emails should be responded to within a day, and during times like teacher and student registration start/end and during the week before the program, the gmail should be checked as much as possible so that time-sensitive matters are resolved (there are inevitably issues where people are having technical glitches that may or may not be our fault). If you are unsure of our policy on something when responding to an email, first check this document, then, if you are still unclear, ask your mentor about it.

### Sending emails {#sending-emails}

For important emails (ie. those being sent to more than 10 people or those responding to a sensitive subject matter), it is always a good idea to have your co-director proof-read your emails. Sending out incorrect information leads to many confused and upset parents, and can also make the day-of more difficult (ie. if school groups show up to the wrong location). Additionally, if you are sent an email that is of importance to the program (ie. a school group cancels last minute), you should be sure to let your co-director know to avoid confusion.

### Clearing the inbox: Archiving and folders {#clearing-the-inbox:-archiving-and-folders}

The email inbox should be empty at all times. This means dealing with the emails in the appropriate manner. There should **never** be opened emails that remain sitting in the inbox unless you have typed up a draft of the response and are waiting for a pending action to be completed (ie. obtaining info from someone about how to respond or having a co-director proofread the email).

There are 5 types of emails/ways to deal with an email:

1. If an email is clearly spam (we get a decent amount of spam) it should be archived.  
2. Emails you have to respond to and DO NOT contain info that you will need to refer back to frequently should be **archived**.  
3. The Splash email receives many emails about Sprout. These should be forwarded to yalesprout@gmail.com when they are received.   
4. Some of the emails in the drive will also be meant for the Splash Presidents (it is usually fairly obvious which emails they are—they are usually about website things or finances). These should be left in the inbox as unread (change the email status to “unread” if you accidentally click on it) so that the Presidents know they have to respond to it. If any of these emails stay in the inbox for multiple days, Slack the Presidents to make sure they are aware of the email/don’t expect the directors to handle the matter.  
5. If the email is something you will have to refer back to later (ie. school group emails, teacher material requests, teacher materials reimbursements, teacher forms, confirmations from the professors teaching at parent program, and anything else that seems important), then it should be placed into the appropriate folder after it is responded to. This makes life way wayyy easier than trying to dig up old emails.

To make this last step easier, at the start of the year you should make the following folders:

Note: As the program goes on, definitely add more folders as needed\!  
   
1\.     Spring 20: School Groups  
2\.     Spring 20: Parent Program Confirmations  
3\.     Spring 20: Teacher Material Requests  
4\.     Spring 20: Teacher Material Reimbursements  
5\.     Spring 20: Teacher Forms

Email Logistics:  
It is a good idea to add the Splash gmail to your phone as you will be using it quite a bit. Also, as the program approaches, I highly suggest you keep a tab on your browser that always has the gmail open, this way you are sure to remember to check it and respond quickly.

For Splash:  
Gmail name: [yalesplash@gmail.com](mailto:yalesplash@gmail.com)   
Gmail password: mollymofbrokencomputers  
For Sprout:  
Gmail name: [yalesprout@gmail.com](mailto:yalesprout@gmail.com)  
Gmail password: danzofmit

### Mail Merge {#mail-merge}

Directors should download the Chrome webapp [“Streak” (click here to go to the chrome web store)](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/streak-crm-for-gmail/pnnfemgpilpdaojpnkjdgfgbnnjojfik) which allows you to send mass emails. While most of the emails will get sent through the website, Streak will allow you to use mail merge (it extracts emails from a .csv file, which is an export format in excel) so you can contact lists of people (ie. one email we send is to all the DUS’s). The basic steps of using Streak to send a message are:

1. Download Streak and log into Streak using the Splash gmail; give it the permissions it asks for.  
2. Generate an excel file that has the emails of all the people you want to email. If you are copying emails in from the Splash website using the “generate a list” function (see the section below that discusses how to get these into a format that works with mail merge). If you want to make the emails more personal, you can add the name of the person next to the their email (in a separate column) as well, and Streak can automatically extract these names and their associated emails/info.  
3. Delete all information on the excel sheet aside from the emails and the names (and headers for the columns). The sheet should look like this:  
   ![][image2]  
4. On excel, go to File\>Save As and save as a .csv file. This is the only file type Streak accepts.  
5. Click compose like you normally would. In the top right corner next to the Cc and Bc buttons, you will see “Mail Merge” in light grey font. Click on that.  
6. An email should open up; in the box at the top, click the blue text saying “Upload .csv” and choose the .csv file appropriately.  
7. Streak will tell you the number of recipients it has found at the top of the email, and lists their names on the right. Double check these (briefly).  
8. If desired, insert a variable, such as the recipients name. To do this, click on the “insert variable” button (see below). Be sure to include Mr./Ms./Professor if appropriate.  
   ![][image3]  
   

### Website Emails {#website-emails}

Generally, it is easier to send emails through the website than through gmail, but there is also a greater chance of having the email being caught by a spam filter when you send emails from the website. The first emails you send will likely be teacher recruitment emails, and you should check to see if those emails are caught by peoples’ spam filters by testing them with admins and yourself (see Avoiding Spam Filters 101 below).

Steps to sending a website email:

1. Go to Manage\>Your Program’s Name\>Email  
2. (a) Use either a Basic List (which can choose basic categories, ie. all students on financial aid) or a Combination list (which can choose complex categories, ie. all parents of students that haven’t paid or submitted financial aid but have signed up for a class)  
   (b) If you’re using a combination list, for example the one listed in parenthesis above, you would start by selecting “Students who signed up for at least one class” in the dropdown menu. Then you would unselect “email the students” and you would instead select “email the students’ guardians.” Then you would select the “AND” and  “NOT” boxes next to “Students who have filled out the credit card form” and “Students who have been granted financial aid” (note that financial aid would have to be updated before you could do this). If needed, you can also add filters using the button on the bottom (this can also be used for generating arbitrary user lists).  
3. Check to make sure the number of users you selected fits what you would expect (it will tell you the number of users at the top)  
4. In the “From” box, type in “yalesplash@gmail.com” (without the quotes). This address will autoforward to the Splash gmail, and it helps avoid spam filters. The “Reply to” box can be left blank  
5. Follow the steps in the “Avoiding Spam Filters 101” below  
6. To add a hyperlink, click on the little chain link thingy (also, usually you will want the link to open in a separate tab, so you should check that box).  
   ![][image4]  
7. ALWAYS double check the text of the email in the “Preview” feature AND make sure DOUBLE CHECK ALL LINKS, even if you already checked them when drafting the email (sometimes HTML is whacky and when you paste in hyperlinks, they get messed up).  
   

### Avoiding Spam Filters 101 {#avoiding-spam-filters-101}

1. Use the insert variable option to add the name of the person the email is being sent to (Note: in the “Preview” it will display the name of the first person on the email list--don’t worry though, it will auto-update for all the other emails)

![][image5]

2. Include the Splash footer (provided in the example below) which has the **street address of LOM**, as well as an **unsubscribe button**  
3. Send the message from yalesplash@gmail.com  
4. Don’t add graphics to the message (ie. a Splash logo)

Example: 

Hello {{user.first\_name}}\!

BODY OF THE EMAIL

**The Splash at Yale Team**   
[yale.learningu.org](http://yale.learningu.org)  
12 Hillhouse Avenue, New Haven, CT, 06520

You received this email because you have signed up for an account on our website, yale.learningu.org. If you received this email multiple times, you may have duplicate accounts. Please email us to delete them. If you no longer wish to receive emails from us, email us or [unsubscribe here](https://yale.learningu.org/myesp/disableaccount).

### Arbitrary User Lists {#arbitrary-user-lists}

1. You will need to use arbitrary user lists to send emails through the gmail; these are often emails to small subsets of the Splash users.   
2. Go to “Manage” and then to your program page. Under “Generate Printables,” go to “Arbitrary User List”  
3. Only select that you want the names and emails, or emails alone  
4. Export to HTML and copy into excel (I’m pretty sure HTML is the better format to choose, but I’m not 100% so just try both the first time you do it)  
5. Use the “text to columns” feature in excel to get the proper format
